1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
10: - Literal input remains until the new word.
11: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
12: - This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
13: 
14: ### Diacritics
15: 
16: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
17: Tones have a separate section.
18: 
19: #### Empty
20: 
21: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
22: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
23:   characters.
24: 
25: #### Circumflex
26: 
27: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
28: 
29: #### Breve
30: 
31: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
32: 
33: #### Horn
34: 
35: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
36: 
37: #### Stroke
38: 
39: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
40: 
41: #### Apply rules
42: 
43: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
44: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
45:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
46:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
47:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
48: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
49:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
50:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
51:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
52: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
53: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
54: 
55: #### Override rules
56: 
57: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
58:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
59:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
60: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
61: - Keep tone untouched.
62: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
63: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
64: 
65: #### Cancel rules
66: 
67: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
68:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
69: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
70:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
71:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
72:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
73:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
74: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
75: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
76:   remains until the new word.
77:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
78: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
79: 
80: #### Auto-fill rules
81: 
82: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
83:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
84: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
85:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
86:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
87:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
88: - The new input character is appended literally.
89: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
90: 
91: ### Tones
92: 
93: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
94: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
95: These vowel could take any tone.
96: 
97: #### Level (ngang)
98: 
99: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
100:   corresponding variants.
101: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
102: 
103: #### Rising (sắc)
104: 
105: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
106: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
107:   -> `Ớ`.
108: 
109: #### Falling (huyền)
110: 
111: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
112: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
113:   -> `Ờ`.
114: 
115: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
116: 
117: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
118: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
119:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
120: 
121: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
122: 
123: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
124: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
125:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
126: 
127: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
128: 
129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
211:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.text:
I've got the context. Here is my proposed approach for the test, written without producing any code.

## Approach

**Goal of the test**

Verify that when the character `F/f`, `J/j`, or `W/w` is appended in `.telex` mode and no diacritic/tone rule applies to it, the engine appends it literally and sets `literal_index = buffer_length_before_add` (i.e., from the new character position onward, processing switches to literal). Cancel cases are excluded per the user's direction; override and apply cases also don't switch to literal, so they're excluded too.

**Test invariants per case (after `add`)**
- `buffer_length == previous_length + 1`
- `buffer_modification_index == null` (no existing span modified)
- `literal_index == previous_length` (new char position)
- `mode == .telex`
- New span: base equals the input, diacritic `.empty`, tone `.level`
- Previously seeded spans unchanged

**Case matrix (other than cancel)**

W/w (breve/horn trigger that doesn't fire)
1. Previous = consonant outside W's scope → e.g. `b`, `g`.
2. Previous = vowel outside W's scope (`E`, `e`, `I`, `i`, `Y`, `y`).
3. Previous = `Ê/ê` (E + circumflex) — not in W's override set.
4. Previous = `Đ/đ` (D + stroke) — outside W's scope.

F/f and J/j (tone trigger that finds no place to put tone)
5. Previous pseudo-word has no vowel — e.g. single consonant `b` or `B`.
6. Previous pseudo-word is exactly `QU/Qu/qU/qu` (QU consonant-only exception).

For each case, sweep both uppercase and lowercase of the trigger character.

**Setup pattern (mirroring existing tests)**

Same shape as `expect State.add cancel existing diacritic for valid cases`: nested `Case` struct, `var state: State = undefined; state.init(); state.mode = .telex; state.buffer_effective[i] = Span.init_diacritic_tone(...); state.buffer_length = N;` then `state.add(new_input);` then assert.

For pseudo-word setup with "qu"/"QU" the buffer needs two seeded spans; for single consonant cases, one span. To keep it clean, split into two sub-loops in the same test: a "single previous span" sub-loop covering W and the F/J consonant-only cases, and a "two previous spans" sub-loop covering the QU exception.

**Cases I think are commonly missed**

- The `QU` exception for F/J — easy to forget because it requires two seeded spans rather than one.
- W after `Ê/ê` and `Đ/đ` — W is "outside scope" not just for non-vowels but also for these diacritic-bearing letters; worth covering since the W branch checks several specific conditions.
- W after `I/i` and `Y/y` — vowels that look like W candidates but aren't in scope.
- Empty-buffer F/J/W is **not** a literal-switch case (no word exists yet), so explicitly exclude it from this test (it belongs to a different test that asserts `literal_index == null`).
- For F/J, we need the seeded spans to all carry `.level` tone; if any vowel has a non-level tone the trigger goes to cancel/override, not literal — easy to misconfigure.

Now consulting the Oracle for an alternative perspective.

I'm planning a single Zig test in src/liblex.zig for a Vietnamese Telex input method. The test name (fixed, do not change) is:

  "expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J, W (ignore cases)"

The spec is specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md. The test focuses on cases where appending F/f, J/j, W/w in `.telex` mode causes the engine to append literally AND set `literal_index = buffer_length_before_add` (switch to literal from that position onward). Cancel cases are explicitly excluded by the user. Apply, override, and auto-fill cases naturally don't switch to literal so they are also excluded.

My planned coverage:

W/w (breve/horn trigger that doesn't fire):
1. Previous span = a consonant outside W's scope (e.g., 'b', 'B', 'g', 'G').
2. Previous span = a vowel outside W's scope ('E','e','I','i','Y','y').
3. Previous span = 'Ê'/'ê' (E with circumflex).
4. Previous span = 'Đ'/'đ' (D with stroke).

5. Previous pseudo-word has no vowel — single consonant (e.g., 'b'/'B').
6. Previous pseudo-word is exactly "QU"/"Qu"/"qU"/"qu" (QU consonant-only exception).

Common assertions per case after `state.add(input)`:
- buffer_length == previous_length + 1
- buffer_modification_index == null
- literal_index == previous_length (new char position)
- mode == .telex

Questions for you:

1. Are there meaningful cases I'm missing for any of F, J, W under the "switch to literal" branch?
2. Any subtle interactions between the literal-switch rule and the tone/diacritic apply, override, or cancel rules I should explicitly test or explicitly exclude?
3. Is the assertion contract (modification_index == null, literal_index == previous_length, last span purely literal, previous spans untouched) the right invariant set per spec?
4. Should this test also cover scenarios where the buffer exceeds the effective buffer length (16) or is that out of scope for this specific test?
5. Any concerns about combining the W cases and F/J cases in one test under a name that says "F, J, W" — or is splitting cleaner?

run.result:
1) TL;DR: Your plan is mostly correct. The main thing I’d add is one multi-consonant no-vowel case for `F/J`, and I would keep buffer-overflow behavior out of this test because it’s a different literal-entry cause.

2) Recommended approach (simple path, S <1h):
1. Keep the single fixed-name test and organize it as two internal groups:
   - `W/w` out-of-scope fallback
   - `F/f` and `J/j` tone-fallback
2. For `W/w`, cover the smallest set of meaningfully distinct cases:
   - one plain out-of-scope previous span
   - one diacritic-bearing out-of-scope previous span
   You do not need every one of `b`, `E`, `Ê`, `Đ` unless you want extra readability.
3. For `F/f` and `J/j`, cover the two real fallback reasons:
   - pseudo-word has no vowel
   - pseudo-word is exactly `QU` in mixed cases
   Add one multi-consonant no-vowel case, not just a single consonant.
4. Keep your common assertions; they are the right contract for this branch.
5. Explicitly exclude:
   - empty buffer
   - already-in-literal cases
   - all apply / override / cancel cases
   - overflow-driven literal mode

3) Rationale and trade-offs:
- Q1: Meaningful missing cases?
  - Yes: for `F/J`, add one no-vowel case with more than one consonant. That validates pseudo-word scanning better than only a single `b/B`.
  - For `W`, I think you are slightly over-covering. In a legal state model, all of your proposed `W` cases reduce to “previous base is not `A/O/U`.” A plain consonant plus one out-of-scope diacritic-bearing span is enough if you want branch coverage with a spec-facing example.
  - `QU` is the important special case for `F/J`; I don’t see another equally important “has vowel but still cannot place tone” case in this spec.
- Q2: Interactions to test or exclude?
  - The key rule is priority: the literal-character rule is lower priority than diacritic/tone rules.
  - So this test should stay strictly on cases where `F/J/W` are appended literally because no higher-priority rule fired.
  - Good exclusions:
    - `W` after `A/O/U` states that apply, override, or cancel
    - `F/J` on any pseudo-word where a tone can be applied
    - `F/J` when the same tone already exists or another tone exists
  - One subtle distinction worth preserving: “appended literally” is not enough; this test is specifically about “appended literally and switched to literal from the new position.”
- Q3: Are your assertions right?
  - Yes. For this branch, the strongest useful invariant set is:
    - `buffer_length == previous_length + 1`
    - `buffer_modification_index == null`
    - `literal_index == previous_length`
    - `mode == .telex`
    - new span is literal (`base=input`, `diacritic=.empty`, `tone=.level`)
    - all seeded earlier spans unchanged
  - That is the correct spec contract for “literal append with new literal switch point.”
- Q4: Include >16 buffer cases?
  - No, out of scope.
  - Overflow is a separate reason for entering literal behavior, and there is already test surface for that.
  - Mixing it here weakens causality: if `literal_index` becomes `16`, you can’t cleanly tell whether you’re validating the `F/J/W` rule or the overflow rule.
- Q5: One test vs split?
  - With the fixed name, one umbrella test is fine.
  - `W` and `F/J` share the same observable contract even though they come from different decision logic.
  - The cleanest compromise is one test with clearly separated case groups and good per-case labels.
  - I would only split later if failures become hard to localize.

4) Risks and guardrails:
- Biggest risk: accidentally choosing a seed that actually belongs to apply/override/cancel, especially for `W`.
- For `F/J`, avoid any pseudo-word that contains a real toneable vowel unless you are deliberately testing the `QU` exception.
- If you keep many `W` cases, the test may become repetitive without adding real branch value.
- If you seed prior spans with tones/diacritics, keep them valid Vietnamese combinations; otherwise you’re testing impossible states.

5) When to consider the advanced path:
- If tone scanning becomes more complex or bug-prone, split out a dedicated `F/J` literal-fallback test later.
- If you see regressions around precedence, add a separate small suite just for “literal rule loses to apply/override/cancel.”
- If boundary bugs appear, add a separate overflow-precedence regression test, not to this one.

6) Optional advanced path:
- A leaner version of this test would treat it as a decision-table test:
  - `W`: out-of-scope previous span
  - `F/J`: no-vowel pseudo-word
  - `F/J`: exact `QU`
- That is enough for branch coverage, while a fuller version like yours is better for spec readability. My bias here is slightly toward leaner `W` coverage and slightly stronger `F/J` scanning coverage.

34: 
35: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
36: 
37: #### Stroke
38: 
39: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
40: 
41: #### Apply rules
42: 
43: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
44: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
45:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
46:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
47:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
48: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
49:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
50:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
51:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
52: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
53: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
54: 
55: #### Override rules
56: 
57: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
58:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
59:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
60: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
61: - Keep tone untouched.
62: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
63: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
64: 
65: #### Cancel rules
66: 
67: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
68:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
69: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
70:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
71:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
72:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
73:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
74: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
75: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
76:   remains until the new word.
77:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
78: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
79: 
80: #### Auto-fill rules
81: 
82: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
83:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
84: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
85:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
86:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
87:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
88: - The new input character is appended literally.
89: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
90: 
91: ### Tones
92: 
93: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
94: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
95: These vowel could take any tone.
96: 
97: #### Level (ngang)
98: 
99: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
100:   corresponding variants.
101: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
102: 
103: #### Rising (sắc)
104: 
105: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
106: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
107:   -> `Ớ`.
108: 
109: #### Falling (huyền)
110: 
111: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
112: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
113:   -> `Ờ`.
114: 
115: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
116: 
117: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
118: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
119:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
120: 
121: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
122: 
123: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
124: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
125:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
126: 
127: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
128: 
129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
211:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR: Your plan is mostly correct. The main thing I’d add is one multi-consonant no-vowel case for `F/J`, and I would keep buffer-overflow behavior out of this test because it’s a different literal-entry cause.

2) Recommended approach (simple path, S <1h):
1. Keep the single fixed-name test and organize it as two internal groups:
   - `W/w` out-of-scope fallback
   - `F/f` and `J/j` tone-fallback
2. For `W/w`, cover the smallest set of meaningfully distinct cases:
   - one plain out-of-scope previous span
   - one diacritic-bearing out-of-scope previous span
   You do not need every one of `b`, `E`, `Ê`, `Đ` unless you want extra readability.
3. For `F/f` and `J/j`, cover the two real fallback reasons:
   - pseudo-word has no vowel
   - pseudo-word is exactly `QU` in mixed cases
   Add one multi-consonant no-vowel case, not just a single consonant.
4. Keep your common assertions; they are the right contract for this branch.
5. Explicitly exclude:
   - empty buffer
   - already-in-literal cases
   - all apply / override / cancel cases
   - overflow-driven literal mode

3) Rationale and trade-offs:
- Q1: Meaningful missing cases?
  - Yes: for `F/J`, add one no-vowel case with more than one consonant. That validates pseudo-word scanning better than only a single `b/B`.
  - For `W`, I think you are slightly over-covering. In a legal state model, all of your proposed `W` cases reduce to “previous base is not `A/O/U`.” A plain consonant plus one out-of-scope diacritic-bearing span is enough if you want branch coverage with a spec-facing example.
  - `QU` is the important special case for `F/J`; I don’t see another equally important “has vowel but still cannot place tone” case in this spec.
- Q2: Interactions to test or exclude?
  - The key rule is priority: the literal-character rule is lower priority than diacritic/tone rules.
  - So this test should stay strictly on cases where `F/J/W` are appended literally because no higher-priority rule fired.
  - Good exclusions:
    - `W` after `A/O/U` states that apply, override, or cancel
    - `F/J` on any pseudo-word where a tone can be applied
    - `F/J` when the same tone already exists or another tone exists
  - One subtle distinction worth preserving: “appended literally” is not enough; this test is specifically about “appended literally and switched to literal from the new position.”
- Q3: Are your assertions right?
  - Yes. For this branch, the strongest useful invariant set is:
    - `buffer_length == previous_length + 1`
    - `buffer_modification_index == null`
    - `literal_index == previous_length`
    - `mode == .telex`
    - new span is literal (`base=input`, `diacritic=.empty`, `tone=.level`)
    - all seeded earlier spans unchanged
  - That is the correct spec contract for “literal append with new literal switch point.”
- Q4: Include >16 buffer cases?
  - No, out of scope.
  - Overflow is a separate reason for entering literal behavior, and there is already test surface for that.
  - Mixing it here weakens causality: if `literal_index` becomes `16`, you can’t cleanly tell whether you’re validating the `F/J/W` rule or the overflow rule.
- Q5: One test vs split?
  - With the fixed name, one umbrella test is fine.
  - `W` and `F/J` share the same observable contract even though they come from different decision logic.
  - The cleanest compromise is one test with clearly separated case groups and good per-case labels.
  - I would only split later if failures become hard to localize.

4) Risks and guardrails:
- Biggest risk: accidentally choosing a seed that actually belongs to apply/override/cancel, especially for `W`.
- For `F/J`, avoid any pseudo-word that contains a real toneable vowel unless you are deliberately testing the `QU` exception.
- If you keep many `W` cases, the test may become repetitive without adding real branch value.
- If you seed prior spans with tones/diacritics, keep them valid Vietnamese combinations; otherwise you’re testing impossible states.

5) When to consider the advanced path:
- If tone scanning becomes more complex or bug-prone, split out a dedicated `F/J` literal-fallback test later.
- If you see regressions around precedence, add a separate small suite just for “literal rule loses to apply/override/cancel.”
- If boundary bugs appear, add a separate overflow-precedence regression test, not to this one.

6) Optional advanced path:
- A leaner version of this test would treat it as a decision-table test:
  - `W`: out-of-scope previous span
  - `F/J`: no-vowel pseudo-word
  - `F/J`: exact `QU`
- That is enough for branch coverage, while a fuller version like yours is better for spec readability. My bias here is slightly toward leaner `W` coverage and slightly stronger `F/J` scanning coverage.

## Refined approach after the Oracle's perspective

Convergence with my plan:
- One umbrella test with the fixed name, organized in two internal groups (W vs F/J).
- Assertion contract is correct: `buffer_length == prev+1`, `buffer_modification_index == null`, `literal_index == prev_length`, `mode == .telex`, last span literal, prior spans unchanged.
- Empty buffer, already-in-literal, apply/override/cancel, and overflow-driven literal are out of scope.

Adjustments to my plan from the Oracle's perspective:
- W coverage: I was over-covering. Trim to one plain out-of-scope previous span (e.g. `b/B`) plus one diacritic-bearing out-of-scope span (e.g. `Ê/ê` or `Đ/đ`). Drop the `E/I/Y` enumeration unless we want extra spec readability.
- F/J coverage: add a multi-consonant no-vowel case (e.g., previous = `n` then `g`) in addition to the single-consonant case, to actually exercise pseudo-word scanning across more than one span.
- Keep the `QU` exception case (in mixed cases for both letters) since it is the only meaningful "has vowel but tone cannot be placed" path in the spec.
- Keep precedence guardrail in mind: every seeded state must NOT match any apply/override/cancel branch for the trigger character — this is the easiest way to accidentally test the wrong branch (especially for W after A/O/U variants).

Final case matrix
- W/w: previous span = `b/B`; previous span = `Ê/ê` (or `Đ/đ`).
- F/f, J/j: previous = single consonant `b/B`; previous = multi-consonant `ng`/`Ng`; previous pseudo-word exactly `QU`/`Qu`/`qU`/`qu`.
- Sweep upper/lowercase of the trigger for each case.

content.text:
## Updated approach with your scope clarifications

Three internal groups under the same fixed test name:

**Group 1 — Empty buffer**
- Trigger char ∈ { `F`, `f`, `J`, `j`, `W`, `w` }, no seeded spans.
- Assertions:
  - `buffer_length == 1`
  - `buffer_modification_index == null`
  - `literal_index == 0` (new char position)
  - `mode == .telex`
  - `buffer_effective[0]`: base = input, diacritic `.empty`, tone `.level`
- Note: current `W` branch checks `buffer_length == 0` and does **not** set `literal_index`, and `F/J` are unimplemented stubs. So these test cases will fail until the implementation is updated. Worth confirming this is the intended behavior change you want the test to drive.

**Group 2 — W cannot apply diacritic (non-empty buffer)**
Cases (each in upper- and lowercase of `W`):
- Previous = consonant outside W's scope: `b`/`B`.
- Previous = vowel outside W's scope: `e`/`E` (representative; could also include `i`/`y` for spec readability).
- Previous = diacritic-bearing letter outside W's scope: `Ê`/`ê`.
- Previous = `Đ`/`đ` (stroke on D).

## My take on splitting

Yes, I think splitting is justified. The three groups differ in three meaningful axes:

| Axis | Group 1 (empty) | Group 2 (W out-of-scope) | Group 3 (F/J no tone) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trigger chars | F, J, W | W only | F, J only |
| Pre-state | none | one seeded span | one or two seeded spans |
| Reason for fallthrough | no word to attach to | base not in W's diacritic scope | pseudo-word has no placeable vowel |
| `literal_index` value | always `0` | `previous_length` | `previous_length` |
| Assertion on prior spans | none (buffer was empty) | one prior span unchanged | one or two prior spans unchanged |

The shared invariant ("appended literally + literal_index set to new-char position") is the same, but the *cause* and *setup shape* differ enough that one big test would mix three Case structs and three sub-loops with different shapes — harder to debug when one branch regresses.

**Proposed split into three tests** (renamed from the original; you said the original name is fixed but you can decide if these refinements are acceptable):

1. `expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J, W on empty buffer`
2. `expect State.add switch to literal input when append W on character outside diacritic scope`
3. `expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J on word without placeable tone`

Each test has one clear cause, one Case struct shape, one sub-loop. Pseudo-word scanning (multi-consonant + QU) stays contained in test 3.

If you want to keep the original single test name, my fallback is one test with three top-level sub-blocks (each its own Case array), separated by comments — workable but noisier than three small tests.

**Confirmations from your last reply, locked in:**
- Empty buffer: `literal_index == 0` is the expected behavior (drives implementation change for W and new code for F/J).
- W Group 2 includes `b/B`, `e/E`, `i/I`, `y/Y`, `Ê/ê`, `Đ/đ`.
- F/J Group 3 multi-consonant: I'll pick 4–5 representatives. Proposal: `ng`, `tr`, `ph`, `kh`, `gh` (common Vietnamese onset clusters).
